turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 17-1094, Nutraceutical Corporation v. Lambert, Justice Sotomayor has the opinion in the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: Troy Lambert sued Nutraceutical Corp. for misleading marketing of dietary supplements. The district court at first allowed Lambert to represent the class of other purchasers, but the court later decertified the class. That is when the deadline we interpret today became relevant. To appeal immediately from an order denying class-action status, Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 23(f) says that a party must ask the Court of Appeals for permission "within 14 days after the order is entered." Lambert, however, took no action until 20 days later when he filed a motion asking the district court to reconsider its decision. He filed a petition for permission to appeal only several months later after the reconsideration motion failed. Nutraceutical objected that Lambert's petition was untimely, but the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit agreed to hear the appeal anyway. Even if the petition was untimely, the Court of Appeals concluded, it could forgive that lateness under the circumstances because Lambert had acted diligently. In other words, the Court of Appeals tolled the deadline for equitable reasons. We hold today, however, that Rule 23(f) is not subject to equitable tolling. It is true as the Court of Appeals concluded that the rule's deadline is not jurisdictional. That means it can be waived or forfeited, but as our opinion explains, the 14-day time limit is nevertheless mandatory when properly raised by an opposing party. We know as much because the governing rules of procedure speak directly to whether Rule 23(f) is flexible. They make clear that it is not. Federal Rules of Appellate Procedure 26(b) is particularly relevant. That rule authorizes extensions of time generally, but with the specific carve out. It says that a Court of Appeals may not extend the time to file a Petition for Permission to Appeal. A Petition for Permission to Appeal is precisely what is at issue here. Lambert suggests the way to read Rule 26(b) more permissively by relying on a distinction that the rule seems to draw between extending a deadline beforehand and accepting a late filing afterward. Unfortunately for Lambert, we already rejected an identical argument in a prior case about a very similarly worded rule and we see no reason why the answer should differ here. When it comes to non-final rulings like the decertification order here, immediate appellate review is the exception not the rule. So it makes sense that Rule 23(f)'s deadline is purposely unforgiving. Lambert's other authorities speak at best indirectly to the timing of petitions for permission to appeal so they cannot dissuade us from heeding the clear guidance of the rules and our precedent. For these and other reasons set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit and remand for further proceedings consistent with this opinion. The decision of the Court is unanimous.
